cases.question: You need to handle several routine PDF jobs back to back — merging two PDFs into one, splitting a PDF into single-page files, and pulling plain text out of a PDF — in an environment where you have a terminal and a general-purpose scripting language available, but no JavaScript runtime. For this kind of everyday, non-form PDF work, name the two general categories of tooling that should be your primary, essential approach (not niche fallbacks) for jobs like these.

You are given a PDF file whose pages are just scanned images (there is no embedded text layer), so a standard text-extraction call on it returns an empty string. Name the two Python function calls — one that rasterizes each PDF page into an image, and one that then runs OCR on that image to recover its text — including the module/package prefix for each. Give your answer as: "<function 1> and <function 2>".

You're building a two-stage PDF pipeline. Stage 1 is a Node.js script using a JavaScript PDF library to load an existing PDF and report how many pages it contains. Stage 2 is a Python script that takes the first page of that same PDF and crops it to a specific region by directly setting the page's boundary-box attributes to left=50, bottom=50, right=550, top=750 (in points), rather than passing a single combined argument. Give (a) the exact method call from stage 1 used to obtain the page count, and (b) the exact four attribute-assignment statements from stage 2 used to perform the crop, in the order left, bottom, right, top.

You need to fill out a scanned, image-only PDF form: because it has no embedded text layer, your plan is to render each page to an image, visually estimate the form field coordinates on those images, and then write out a coordinates file for the fill step. Your organization's policy forbids adding any GPL-licensed component to this pipeline. Based only on the licensing information available to you, name a Python library suitable for the PDF-to-image rendering step along with its stated license, and give the exact pair of JSON key names used (instead of the PDF-point equivalents) in the coordinates file's per-page dimension entry to signal that the coordinates that follow are in image-pixel space.
